title: Intersection Theory of Matroids: Variations on a Theme
speaker: Federico Ardila
abstract: Chow rings of toric varieties feature a rich combinatorial structure of independent interest. We will begin by surveying four different ways of computing in these rings, due to Billera, Brion, Fulton–Sturmfels, and Allermann–Rau. We will illustrate the beauty and power of these methods by sketching four proofs of Huh and Huh–Katz’s formula μk(M)=degM(α∗r−kβk) for the coefficients of the reduced characteristic polynomial of a matroid M as the mixed intersection numbers of the hyperplane and reciprocal hyperplane classes α and β in the Chow ring of M. Each of these proofs sheds light on a different aspect of matroid combinatorics, and provides a framework for more recent developments in the intersection theory of matroids.
tags: Workshop on Combinatorics of Enumerative Geometry

proof_stages.label: Motivating intersection numbers from chromatic and classical geometry
proof_stages.summary: The talk frames chromatic-polynomial log-concavity and classical curve intersections as instances of the problem of interpreting and computing intersection numbers in Chow rings.

proof_stages.label: Toric and permutohedral reduction of Chow computations
proof_stages.summary: The speaker restricts to toric varieties, especially the permutohedral toric variety, where the fan combinatorics of rays, cones, and flags makes Chow-ring computations accessible.

proof_stages.label: Three computational models for toric Chow rings
proof_stages.summary: Danilov-Brion generators and relations, Billera piecewise-polynomial splines, and Fulton-Sturmfels Minkowski weights are presented as equivalent but practically different calculi for multiplication and vanishing in toric Chow rings.

proof_stages.label: Hodge package and the need to identify degrees
proof_stages.summary: The Lefschetz and Hodge-Riemann formalism supplies inequalities such as log-concavity once the correct classes are chosen, so the main combinatorial burden is to compute the resulting degree numbers.

proof_stages.label: Matroids as Bergman fans and tropical linear spaces
proof_stages.summary: Matroids are recalled via bases and flats, their Bergman fans are embedded in the permutohedral fan, and tropical balancing and degree-one tropical fans explain why matroids are tropical analogues of linear spaces.

proof_stages.label: Characteristic-polynomial coefficients as matroid intersection degrees
proof_stages.summary: The coefficients of the characteristic polynomial are identified with intersections of the matroid fan against powers of the tropical hyperplane classes alpha and beta, and the speaker sketches proofs using each of the computational models.

proof_stages.label: Recent variations: CSM, conormal, likelihood, and augmented theories
proof_stages.summary: The final part surveys newer matroid intersection computations—CSM cycles, conormal fans and pushforwards, likelihood-degree/tropical-critical-point calculations, and augmented or arboreal enumerations—emphasizing how different calculi reveal different combinatorics.
